$(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_bench_ref.elf: $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_bench.o
	$$(MSG_LD)$$(Q)$$(LD) -o $$@ $$^ $$(LDFLAGS)

bench-$(1): $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_bench_opt.elf $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_bench_ref.elf
	@$$(GDB) $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_bench_ref.elf $$(FPGA_GDB_FLAGS) > $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_bench_ref.txt 2>&1; \
	 $$(GDB) $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_bench_opt.elf $$(FPGA_GDB_FLAGS) > $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_bench_opt.txt 2>&1; \
	 awk 'FNR==1{f++} /The cycle count is/{if(f==1)rc=$$$$5;else oc=$$$$5} /The inst count is/{if(f==1)ri=$$$$5;else oi=$$$$5} \
	      END{sp=(oc>0)?rc/oc:0; printf "  %-16s %8d %8d %7.2fx %8d %8d\n","$(1)",rc,oc,sp,ri,oi}' \
	      $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_bench_ref.txt $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_bench_opt.txt
endef

define FUNC_RULES_BENCH_DOUBLE_OPT
$(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_bench_opt.elf: $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_bench.o s_$(1).o libandes.a
	$$(MSG_LD)$$(Q)$$(LD) -o $$@ $$(filter-out libandes.a,$$^) $$(LDFLAGS_OPT)

$(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_bench_ref.elf: $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_bench.o
	$$(MSG_LD)$$(Q)$$(LD) -o $$@ $$^ $$(LDFLAGS)

bench-$(1): $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_bench_opt.elf $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_bench_ref.elf
	@$$(GDB) $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_bench_ref.elf $$(FPGA_GDB_FLAGS) > $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_bench_ref.txt 2>&1; \
	 $$(GDB) $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_bench_opt.elf $$(FPGA_GDB_FLAGS) > $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_bench_opt.txt 2>&1; \
	 awk 'FNR==1{f++} /The cycle count is/{if(f==1)rc=$$$$5;else oc=$$$$5} /The inst count is/{if(f==1)ri=$$$$5;else oi=$$$$5} \
	      END{sp=(oc>0)?rc/oc:0; printf "  %-16s %8d %8d %7.2fx %8d %8d\n","$(1)",rc,oc,sp,ri,oi}' \
	      $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_bench_ref.txt $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_bench_opt.txt
endef

$(foreach f,$(FUNCS_SINGLE),$(eval $(call FUNC_RULES_BENCH_SINGLE,$(f))))
$(foreach f,$(FUNCS_DOUBLE_OPT),$(eval $(call FUNC_RULES_BENCH_DOUBLE_OPT,$(f))))

# --- Top-level targets ---

OPT_ELFS = $(foreach f,$(FUNCS_SINGLE),$(TESTDIR)/test_$(f)_opt.elf) \
           $(foreach f,$(FUNCS_DOUBLE_OPT),$(TESTDIR)/test_$(f)_opt.elf)
REF_ELFS = $(foreach f,$(FUNCS),$(TESTDIR)/test_$(f)_ref.elf)

all: $(OPT_ELFS) $(REF_ELFS)

test-all: $(foreach f,$(FUNCS),test-$(f))
	@echo "Done."

content.text:
The output columns are: `ref_cyc  opt_cyc  speedup` where speedup = ref/opt. With 0.81x, opt (153843) is **slower** than ref (125372).

**Root cause: `call __clzsi2` in the hot path (line 57), plus a double stack frame manipulation around it.**

For the bench input `2.5f + i` (i=0..99), every iteration has a non-zero fractional part, so the code always hits the normalization path at lines 48-67:

1. **Extra 32-byte stack frame** is allocated (line 49), 6 caller-saved registers are saved (lines 50-55), `__clzsi2` is called, then restored (lines 59-64), then frame freed (line 65) - that's **12 load/store instructions + function call overhead** every iteration, on top of the outer frame.

2. **`call __clzsi2`** itself: function call with branch, link, return overhead, plus potential icache miss fetching the CLZ implementation from a distant address.

The ref (newlib) presumably implements `modff` without needing CLZ at all, or with a different algorithm that avoids this call.

The fix is to **inline the CLZ** - eliminate lines 49-65 entirely and replace the `call __clzsi2` with a ~10-15 instruction binary-search CLZ sequence. Even though that adds instructions (explaining the slightly lower opt inst count vs ref: 24260 vs 24560), it removes the function call overhead and the 12 extra memory accesses, which is a net win in cycles.

Want me to implement the inline CLZ?

content.text:
Correctness passes. Results:

| | ref_cyc | opt_cyc | speedup | ref_ins | opt_ins |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| before | 125372 | 153843 | 0.81x | 24560 | 24260 |
| after | 125258 | 101735 | **1.23x** | 24560 | 22700 |

0.81x -> 1.23x. The inlined CLZ eliminates 12 load/store instructions and a function call per iteration, replacing them with register-only binary-search CLZ. The frame shrink (32 -> 16 bytes) also removes `sw ra` / `lw ra`.

message.content: review other functions to see if we could optimize for latency instead of code size

input.description: Review RISC-V libm asm for latency bottlenecks
input.prompt:
Analyze all assembly (.s) files in /home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/libm/machine/riscv/ (NOT subdirectories) for latency bottlenecks. Look specifically for:

1. Calls to helper functions (call, jal, jalr, tail used as call) - especially __clzsi2, __clzdi2, or any __*si2/__*di2 libgcc functions, or any __gf_* calls that happen inside a function's hot path
2. Patterns where registers are saved to stack, a function is called, then registers are restored (double-stack-frame or push/call/pop patterns) - this is expensive
3. Any other obvious latency issues (unnecessary branches, redundant memory ops in hot paths)

For each issue found, report:
- File name
- Which function it's in
- Line numbers of the problematic pattern
- What the pattern is (e.g., "call __clzsi2 with 12-instruction push/pop wrapper")

Be thorough - check every .s file in the directory. Do NOT look in subdirectories like rv32e/.

Also note: the sf_modf.s file has already been optimized (inline CLZ replacing call __clzsi2), so skip that one.

# Latency Bottleneck Analysis: __clzsi2 Call Wrappers in libm/machine/riscv/*.s

## Pattern Summary

Every `__clzsi2` callsite in the top-level directory uses an identical anti-pattern: a 32-byte stack frame is allocated, 5-6 caller-save registers are spilled to memory, the call is made, then all registers are reloaded and the frame torn down. The hardware CLZ-equivalent (already done inline in `sf_modf.s` lines 48-73) is roughly 19 instructions with no memory traffic. The wrapper pattern adds ~14 memory ops + frame adjustments per call, plus the actual `__clzsi2` body cost (a software loop, since these builds are RV32IM without Zbb).

The `andes_dpexlog.s` and `andes_fpexlog.s` files even define a private `.Ldo_clzsi2` / `.Ldo_clzsi2_f` helper that does its own push/call/pop, and then *call that helper* from the math routine - which itself is also called from a save/restore wrapper. Effectively a double-wrapped CLZ.

## Per-File Findings

| File | Function (label scope) | Lines | Pattern |
|---|---|---|---|
| `s_acos.s` | `acos` | 55-69 | -32 frame, spill x10-x12,x14,x15, call __clzsi2, restore, +32 |
| `s_asin.s` | `asin` | 45-61 | -32 frame, spill x10-x15 (pushm r0,r5), call, restore, +32 |
| `s_atan2.s` | `atan2` (LL10) | 184-200 | Two-stage push: -16 (x10,x11), then -16 (x13-x15), call __clzsi2, restore both, +16 +16 |
| `s_atan2.s` | `atan2` (LL13) | 248-264 | Same two-stage push/pop wrapper around __clzsi2 |
| `s_fmod.s` | `fmod` (LL4) | 94-108 | -32 frame, spill x10-x12,x14,x15, call __clzsi2, restore, +32 |
| `s_fmod.s` | `fmod` (LL10) | 179-193 | Same wrapper, second occurrence |
| `s_fmod.s` | `fmod` (LL13) | 223-237 | Same wrapper, third occurrence |
| `s_log.s` | `log` (LL11) | 87-103 | -32 frame, spill x10-x15 (pushm r0,r5), call __clzsi2, restore, +32 |
| `s_log10.s` | `log10` (LL17) | 99-115 | -32 frame, spill x10-x15, call __clzsi2, restore, +32 |
| `s_modf.s` | `modf` (LL19) | 54-67 | -32 frame, spill x11-x15, call __clzsi2, restore, +32 |
| `s_pow.s` | `pow` (LL25) | 242-258 | -32 frame, spill x10-x15, call __clzsi2, restore, +32 |
| `s_sqrt.s` | `sqrt` | 242-258 | -32 frame, spill x10-x15, call __clzsi2, restore, +32 |
| `sf_acos.s` | `acosf` (Li34) | 38-52 | -16 frame, spill x14,x15, call __clzsi2, then call __gf_fpexsqrt, restore, +16. Note both calls share one wrapper - acceptable |
| `sf_acos.s` | `acosf` (Li44) | 99-102 | Bare `call __clzsi2` with no save/restore (already inside larger function frame) - acceptable |
| `sf_asin.s` | `asinf` | 46-59 | -16 frame, spill x14,x15, call __clzsi2, then __gf_fpexsqrt, restore, +16. Same shared wrapper as sf_acos |
| `sf_fmod.s` | `fmodf` (Li3 path) | 53-69 | -32 frame, spill x10-x15 (pushm r0,r5), call __clzsi2, restore, +32 |
| `sf_fmod.s` | `fmodf` (LAspecA) | 100-116 | Same wrapper |
| `sf_fmod.s` | `fmodf` (LAspecB) | 124-140 | Same wrapper |
| `sf_frexp.s` | `frexpf` | 33-49 | Two-stage: -16 (x10-x13), then -16 (x15), call __clzsi2, restore both, +16 +16 |
| `sf_ldexp.s` | `ldexpf` | 52-71 | Two-stage push/pop wrapping call __clzsi2 (similar to sf_frexp) |
| `sf_log.s` | `logf` (LBspec) | 46-62 | Two-stage: -16 (x10-x13), -16 (x15), call __clzsi2, restore both, +16 +16 |
| `sf_log10.s` | `log10f` (LCspec2) | 55-71 | -32 frame, spill x10-x15, call __clzsi2, restore, +32 |
| `sf_pow.s` | `powf` (LAspecA) | 214-230 | -32 frame, spill x10-x15, call __clzsi2, restore, +32 |
| `sf_sinh.s` | `sinhf` | 113-129 | -32 frame, spill x10-x15, call __clzsi2, restore, +32 |
| `andes_exsub.s` | `__gf_exsub` | 45-61 | -32 frame, spill x10-x15, call __clzsi2, restore, +32 |
| `andes_dpexatan.s` | `__gf_dpexatan` | 220-236 | -32 frame, spill x10-x15, call __clzsi2, restore, +32 |
| `andes_dpexlog.s` | `__gf_dpexlog` -> `.Ldo_clzsi2` | 284-303 | Private helper does its own -24 frame, spills x10-x14 + ra, calls __clzsi2, restores. Caller (line ~not shown) jumps in via `call .Ldo_clzsi2`, so double-frame around __clzsi2 |
| `andes_fpexlog.s` | `__gf_fpexlog` -> `.Ldo_clzsi2_f` | 252-273 | Private helper does its own -28 frame, spills x10-x15 + ra, calls __clzsi2. Same double-wrapper issue |

## Other Helper Calls in Hot Paths

These are not __clzsi2 but are also expensive helper calls in hot math routines (called via `call` from inside math functions, often soft-float libgcc):

- `s_cbrt.s` lines 66, 84, 92, 110, 130 - five `call __divdf3` and one `call __subdf3` in `cbrt` body
- `s_nextafter.s` lines 42, 82, 85, 109, 114, 128 - `__nedf2`, `__muldf3`, `__eqdf2`, `__adddf3`
- `e_acos.s` lines 81, 101, 129, 151 - `__divdf3`, `__ieee754_sqrt`
- `k_rem_pio2.s` lines 87, 125, 154, 155, 165, 166, 176, 196, 199, 201, 206, 247, 300, 312, 320, 368, 387, 411 - massive number of soft-float helper calls (`__floatsidf`, `__adddf3`, `__fixdfsi`, `__subdf3`, `__gedf2`, `__eqdf2`)
- `e_jn.s` (and presumably `e_yn.s`) - dozens of soft-float helper calls in hot loops
- `andes_r.s` line 49 - `call __divdf3`

These soft-float calls are harder to eliminate without rewriting the routines, but they share the property that the math function spills caller-saves to call libgcc - latency is dominated by the soft-float helper itself, not the wrapper.

## Other Latency Issues Observed

- `s_modf.s` line 54: `addi sp, sp, -32` allocates a *second* stack frame on top of the function's existing frame just to spill 5 regs around the call. Net frame growth is 64 bytes for one CLZ.
- `s_atan2.s` lines 184/187 and 248/251, `sf_frexp.s` lines 33/38, `sf_log.s` lines 46/51, `sf_ldexp.s`: split into two `addi sp,sp,-16` pairs (legacy translation of nds32 `pushm` instructions). Each pair is 1 extra `addi` than necessary - merging into a single -32 saves 2 instructions.
- `andes_dpexlog.s` / `andes_fpexlog.s` private CLZ helpers: every caller pays the full call+ret overhead plus the helper's own 12-instruction spill/restore, then __clzsi2's own prologue/epilogue. Triple overhead.

## Impact

For RV32IM without Zbb, `__clzsi2` itself is a software loop (roughly 30-50 cycles). The wrapper adds ~14 memory ops (~14-28 cycles depending on cache) + ~4 frame instructions + call/ret latency (~6 cycles). Replacing each call with the inline 19-instruction CLZ from `sf_modf.s` should save roughly 30-60 cycles per call on FPGA, with significant variation based on data cache hit/miss for the spill slots.

The highest-impact targets (called from common hot paths and called multiple times per invocation) are:
1. `s_fmod.s` / `sf_fmod.s` - 3 callsites each in fmod
2. `s_atan2.s` - 2 callsites in atan2
3. `andes_dpexlog.s` / `andes_fpexlog.s` - the double-wrapped helpers feed `log`, `pow`, `atanh`, etc.
4. `s_log.s` / `sf_log.s` / `s_log10.s` / `sf_log10.s` - log family
5. `s_pow.s` / `sf_pow.s` - pow family

- `e_acos.s` lines 81, 101, 129, 151 - `__divdf3`, `__ieee754_sqrt`
- `k_rem_pio2.s` lines 87, 125, 154, 155, 165, 166, 176, 196, 199, 201, 206, 247, 300, 312, 320, 368, 387, 411 - massive number of soft-float helper calls (`__floatsidf`, `__adddf3`, `__fixdfsi`, `__subdf3`, `__gedf2`, `__eqdf2`)

- `andes_r.s` line 49 - `call __divdf3`

These soft-float calls are harder to eliminate without rewriting the routines, but they share the property that the math function spills caller-saves to call libgcc - latency is dominated by the soft-float helper itself, not the wrapper.

## Other Latency Issues Observed

- `s_modf.s` line 54: `addi sp, sp, -32` allocates a *second* stack frame on top of the function's existing frame just to spill 5 regs around the call. Net frame growth is 64 bytes for one CLZ.
- `s_atan2.s` lines 184/187 and 248/251, `sf_frexp.s` lines 33/38, `sf_log.s` lines 46/51, `sf_ldexp.s`: split into two `addi sp,sp,-16` pairs (legacy translation of nds32 `pushm` instructions). Each pair is 1 extra `addi` than necessary - merging into a single -32 saves 2 instructions.
- `andes_dpexlog.s` / `andes_fpexlog.s` private CLZ helpers: every caller pays the full call+ret overhead plus the helper's own 12-instruction spill/restore, then __clzsi2's own prologue/epilogue. Triple overhead.

## Impact

For RV32IM without Zbb, `__clzsi2` itself is a software loop (roughly 30-50 cycles). The wrapper adds ~14 memory ops (~14-28 cycles depending on cache) + ~4 frame instructions + call/ret latency (~6 cycles). Replacing each call with the inline 19-instruction CLZ from `sf_modf.s` should save roughly 30-60 cycles per call on FPGA, with significant variation based on data cache hit/miss for the spill slots.

The highest-impact targets (called from common hot paths and called multiple times per invocation) are:
1. `s_fmod.s` / `sf_fmod.s` - 3 callsites each in fmod
2. `s_atan2.s` - 2 callsites in atan2
3. `andes_dpexlog.s` / `andes_fpexlog.s` - the double-wrapped helpers feed `log`, `pow`, `atanh`, etc.
4. `s_log.s` / `sf_log.s` / `s_log10.s` / `sf_log10.s` - log family
5. `s_pow.s` / `sf_pow.s` - pow family

# Inline CLZ across RISC-V libm assembly for latency

## Context

`sf_modf.s` was found to be 23% slower than newlib's C reference on the Andes
FPGA (125k vs 154k cycles) despite having fewer total instructions. Root cause:
a `call __clzsi2` wrapped in a push/pop block that saved 6 caller-save
registers to the stack around the call. Replacing that with an inline
binary-search CLZ (19 instructions, no memory traffic) flipped the result to
1.23x faster (101k cycles).

The same anti-pattern exists in 26 other callsites across the top-level
`libm/machine/riscv/*.s` files. This plan inlines CLZ everywhere it appears in
the hot path, prioritising latency over code size.

## Scope

**In scope:** every `call __clzsi2` in `libm/machine/riscv/*.s` (top-level
only; `rv32e/` is a separate concern and generally build-optimised for size).

**Out of scope:**
- Soft-float helper calls (`__divdf3`, `__adddf3`, `__muldf3`, `__fixdfsi`,
  etc.) in `k_rem_pio2.s`, `e_jn.s`, `s_cbrt.s`, `s_nextafter.s`, `e_acos.s`.
  Inlining these is a much larger rewrite and belongs in a separate pass.
- `rv32e/` subdirectory.
- `sf_modf.s` (already done).

## Target files (26 callsites)

High-impact (called from common hot paths, some multiple times per function):

1. `andes_dpexlog.s` - 2 calls via private `.Ldo_clzsi2` helper (feeds log,
   pow, atanh, expm1, etc. for double precision)
2. `andes_fpexlog.s` - 2 calls via private `.Ldo_clzsi2_f` helper (float
   counterpart)
3. `s_fmod.s` - 3 callsites (LL4 @94, LL10 @179, LL13 @223)
4. `sf_fmod.s` - 3 callsites (Li3 @53, LAspecA @100, LAspecB @124)
5. `s_atan2.s` - 2 callsites (LL10 @184, LL13 @248), each using two-stage push
6. `s_log.s` - 1 callsite (LL11 @87)
7. `s_log10.s` - 1 callsite (LL17 @99)
8. `sf_log.s` - 1 callsite (LBspec @46), two-stage push
9. `sf_log10.s` - 1 callsite (LCspec2 @55)
10. `s_pow.s` - 1 callsite (LL25 @242)
11. `sf_pow.s` - 1 callsite (LAspecA @214)
12. `s_sqrt.s` - 1 callsite @242
13. `s_acos.s` - 1 callsite @55
14. `s_asin.s` - 1 callsite @45
15. `s_modf.s` - 1 callsite (LL19 @54) - double-frame case
16. `sf_sinh.s` - 1 callsite @113
17. `sf_frexp.s` - 1 callsite @33, two-stage push
18. `sf_ldexp.s` - 1 callsite @52, two-stage push
19. `andes_exsub.s` - 1 callsite @45 (shared helper, called from `__gf_exadd`,
    `__gf_exmul` via exsub path)
20. `andes_dpexatan.s` - 1 callsite @220

Already-acceptable, do not touch:
- `sf_acos.s` lines 38-52 and 99-102, `sf_asin.s` lines 46-59 - wrapper is
  shared with an adjacent `__gf_fpexsqrt` call, so the spill is amortised.

## The inline CLZ template (reusable)

This is the sequence already deployed in `sf_modf.s`. It assumes the input is
non-zero (all current callsites have a `beqz ..., <skip>` immediately before).
Adjust register names per callsite - input register, output register, and two
scratch registers must be chosen based on what is live at that point.

```asm
	# CLZ of <IN> -> <OUT>; input must be non-zero
	# scratch: <SCR1> (copy of IN), <SCR2> (mask/flag)
	li	<OUT>, 0
	mv	<SCR1>, <IN>
	srli	<SCR2>, <SCR1>, 16
	bnez	<SCR2>, .Lclz8_<tag>
	slli	<SCR1>, <SCR1>, 16
	addi	<OUT>, <OUT>, 16
.Lclz8_<tag>:
	srli	<SCR2>, <SCR1>, 24
	bnez	<SCR2>, .Lclz4_<tag>
	slli	<SCR1>, <SCR1>, 8
	addi	<OUT>, <OUT>, 8
.Lclz4_<tag>:
	srli	<SCR2>, <SCR1>, 28
	bnez	<SCR2>, .Lclz2_<tag>
	slli	<SCR1>, <SCR1>, 4
	addi	<OUT>, <OUT>, 4
.Lclz2_<tag>:
	srli	<SCR2>, <SCR1>, 30
	bnez	<SCR2>, .Lclz1_<tag>
	slli	<SCR1>, <SCR1>, 2
	addi	<OUT>, <OUT>, 2
.Lclz1_<tag>:
	srli	<SCR2>, <SCR1>, 31
	xori	<SCR2>, <SCR2>, 1
	add	<OUT>, <OUT>, <SCR2>
```

`<tag>` is a unique per-callsite suffix (e.g. `_fmod1`, `_log`, `_atan2_10`)
to avoid label collisions when a file has multiple callsites.

`<SCR1>` must be preserved across the sequence; `<IN>` is untouched if it's
not the same register as `<SCR1>`. Callsites that need `<IN>` preserved after
CLZ must use a distinct `<SCR1>`. Callsites that immediately do
`sll <IN>, <IN>, <OUT>` right after can use `<IN>` itself as `<SCR1>`.

## Per-file actions

For each target file:

1. Read the file.
2. Locate the `addi sp, sp, -N` / spill / `call __clzsi2` / reload /
   `addi sp, sp, +N` block.
3. Identify which registers are live at that point and pick 2 dead registers
   for `<SCR1>`/`<SCR2>`. Common free registers: `t0` (x5), `t1` (x6), `t2`
   (x7), `t6` (x31), `x17` (a7). Verify by grep that the chosen scratch is not
   referenced between the end of the CLZ and the next assignment.
4. Replace the push/call/pop block with the inline CLZ template, substituting
   `<IN>` = the register that was `mv x10, <reg>`'d before the call, `<OUT>` =
   the register that received the `mv ..., x10` after the call.
5. If the CLZ was the only `call` in the function, also remove `sw ra, ...` /
   `lw ra, ...` from the outer prologue/epilogue and shrink that frame by 4
   bytes (or 0 if the remaining saved regs already fit).
6. If the file has a two-stage push (two `addi sp, sp, -16` pairs), both
   disappear along with the call they wrapped.

### Special case: `andes_dpexlog.s` / `andes_fpexlog.s`

These define a private helper `.Ldo_clzsi2` (`.Ldo_clzsi2_f`) with its own
`-24`/`-28` frame that spills x10-x14(+x15), calls `__clzsi2`, and returns.
Each helper is called from exactly 2 sites via `call .Ldo_clzsi2`.

Two options:
- **A (chosen):** inline the CLZ body at each of the 2 callsites (4 inlinings
  total across both files) and delete the helper function. Maximum latency
  reduction: no call+ret, no memory traffic, no __clzsi2 body.
- B: leave the helper, but replace its body with inline CLZ. Simpler change,
  keeps one call+ret of overhead.

Going with **A** because the user asked to optimise for latency.

### Special case: `andes_exsub.s`

`__gf_exsub` is itself a shared helper called from `__gf_exadd`, `__gf_exmul`,
`__gf_dpexatan`, etc. The `call __clzsi2` inside `__gf_exsub` has its own
register pressure - need to verify which registers it saves in its own
prologue and pick scratch from there.

## Verification

After each file is edited (or in batches):

```sh
cd libm/machine/riscv
make test-<func>        # correctness vs newlib reference
make bench-<func>       # cycle count opt vs ref on FPGA
```

Final full-suite verification:

```sh
make test-all           # every function passes diff
make bench-all          # full cycle/instruction table
```

Record before/after numbers for each touched function. A non-regressing file
must: (a) still pass `test-<func>`, (b) show opt_cyc <= previous opt_cyc, and
(c) show speedup >= 1.00x (opt no slower than ref). The 1.23x result for
`modff` is the baseline expectation for best-case improvement.

## Critical files to modify

- `libm/machine/riscv/andes_dpexlog.s`
- `libm/machine/riscv/andes_fpexlog.s`
- `libm/machine/riscv/andes_exsub.s`
- `libm/machine/riscv/andes_dpexatan.s`
- `libm/machine/riscv/s_fmod.s`
- `libm/machine/riscv/sf_fmod.s`
- `libm/machine/riscv/s_atan2.s`
- `libm/machine/riscv/s_log.s`
- `libm/machine/riscv/s_log10.s`
- `libm/machine/riscv/sf_log.s`
- `libm/machine/riscv/sf_log10.s`
- `libm/machine/riscv/s_pow.s`
- `libm/machine/riscv/sf_pow.s`
- `libm/machine/riscv/s_sqrt.s`
- `libm/machine/riscv/s_acos.s`
- `libm/machine/riscv/s_asin.s`
- `libm/machine/riscv/s_modf.s`
- `libm/machine/riscv/sf_sinh.s`
- `libm/machine/riscv/sf_frexp.s`
- `libm/machine/riscv/sf_ldexp.s`

## Reference

Working example of the pattern: `libm/machine/riscv/sf_modf.s` lines 48-76
(inline CLZ using `t6`/`x17` as scratch, frame shrunk from 32 to 16 bytes,
`ra` save removed).

## Execution order (suggested)

1. Start with leaf/shared helpers that feed many math routines. Fixing these
   improves everything downstream:
   - `andes_dpexlog.s`, `andes_fpexlog.s` (feeds log/pow/expm1 family)
   - `andes_exsub.s` (feeds extended-precision arithmetic)
   - `andes_dpexatan.s` (feeds atan family)
2. Then the direct math routines that still have their own CLZ calls:
   `s_fmod.s`, `sf_fmod.s`, `s_atan2.s`, etc.
3. Finally the simpler one-offs: frexp, ldexp, sinh, sqrt, etc.
4. After each file, run `make test-<f>` and `make bench-<f>`; after the full
   batch, run `make test-all` and `make bench-all`.

# Inline CLZ across RISC-V libm assembly for latency

## Context

`sf_modf.s` was found to be 23% slower than newlib's C reference on the Andes
FPGA (125k vs 154k cycles) despite having fewer total instructions. Root cause:
a `call __clzsi2` wrapped in a push/pop block that saved 6 caller-save
registers to the stack around the call. Replacing that with an inline
binary-search CLZ (19 instructions, no memory traffic) flipped the result to
1.23x faster (101k cycles).

The same anti-pattern exists in 26 other callsites across the top-level
`libm/machine/riscv/*.s` files. This plan inlines CLZ everywhere it appears in
the hot path, prioritising latency over code size.

## Scope

**In scope:** every `call __clzsi2` in `libm/machine/riscv/*.s` (top-level
only; `rv32e/` is a separate concern and generally build-optimised for size).

**Out of scope:**
- Soft-float helper calls (`__divdf3`, `__adddf3`, `__muldf3`, `__fixdfsi`,
  etc.) in `k_rem_pio2.s`, `e_jn.s`, `s_cbrt.s`, `s_nextafter.s`, `e_acos.s`.
  Inlining these is a much larger rewrite and belongs in a separate pass.
- `rv32e/` subdirectory.
- `sf_modf.s` (already done).

## Target files (26 callsites)

High-impact (called from common hot paths, some multiple times per function):

1. `andes_dpexlog.s` - 2 calls via private `.Ldo_clzsi2` helper (feeds log,
   pow, atanh, expm1, etc. for double precision)
2. `andes_fpexlog.s` - 2 calls via private `.Ldo_clzsi2_f` helper (float
   counterpart)
3. `s_fmod.s` - 3 callsites (LL4 @94, LL10 @179, LL13 @223)
4. `sf_fmod.s` - 3 callsites (Li3 @53, LAspecA @100, LAspecB @124)
5. `s_atan2.s` - 2 callsites (LL10 @184, LL13 @248), each using two-stage push
6. `s_log.s` - 1 callsite (LL11 @87)
7. `s_log10.s` - 1 callsite (LL17 @99)
8. `sf_log.s` - 1 callsite (LBspec @46), two-stage push
9. `sf_log10.s` - 1 callsite (LCspec2 @55)
10. `s_pow.s` - 1 callsite (LL25 @242)
11. `sf_pow.s` - 1 callsite (LAspecA @214)
12. `s_sqrt.s` - 1 callsite @242
13. `s_acos.s` - 1 callsite @55
14. `s_asin.s` - 1 callsite @45
15. `s_modf.s` - 1 callsite (LL19 @54) - double-frame case
16. `sf_sinh.s` - 1 callsite @113
17. `sf_frexp.s` - 1 callsite @33, two-stage push
18. `sf_ldexp.s` - 1 callsite @52, two-stage push
19. `andes_exsub.s` - 1 callsite @45 (shared helper, called from `__gf_exadd`,
    `__gf_exmul` via exsub path)
20. `andes_dpexatan.s` - 1 callsite @220

Already-acceptable, do not touch:
- `sf_acos.s` lines 38-52 and 99-102, `sf_asin.s` lines 46-59 - wrapper is
  shared with an adjacent `__gf_fpexsqrt` call, so the spill is amortised.

## The inline CLZ template (reusable)

This is the sequence already deployed in `sf_modf.s`. It assumes the input is
non-zero (all current callsites have a `beqz ..., <skip>` immediately before).
Adjust register names per callsite - input register, output register, and two
scratch registers must be chosen based on what is live at that point.

```asm
	# CLZ of <IN> -> <OUT>; input must be non-zero
	# scratch: <SCR1> (copy of IN), <SCR2> (mask/flag)
	li	<OUT>, 0
	mv	<SCR1>, <IN>
	srli	<SCR2>, <SCR1>, 16
	bnez	<SCR2>, .Lclz8_<tag>
	slli	<SCR1>, <SCR1>, 16
	addi	<OUT>, <OUT>, 16
.Lclz8_<tag>:
	srli	<SCR2>, <SCR1>, 24
	bnez	<SCR2>, .Lclz4_<tag>
	slli	<SCR1>, <SCR1>, 8
	addi	<OUT>, <OUT>, 8
.Lclz4_<tag>:
	srli	<SCR2>, <SCR1>, 28
	bnez	<SCR2>, .Lclz2_<tag>
	slli	<SCR1>, <SCR1>, 4
	addi	<OUT>, <OUT>, 4
.Lclz2_<tag>:
	srli	<SCR2>, <SCR1>, 30
	bnez	<SCR2>, .Lclz1_<tag>
	slli	<SCR1>, <SCR1>, 2
	addi	<OUT>, <OUT>, 2
.Lclz1_<tag>:
	srli	<SCR2>, <SCR1>, 31
	xori	<SCR2>, <SCR2>, 1
	add	<OUT>, <OUT>, <SCR2>
```

`<tag>` is a unique per-callsite suffix (e.g. `_fmod1`, `_log`, `_atan2_10`)
to avoid label collisions when a file has multiple callsites.

`<SCR1>` must be preserved across the sequence; `<IN>` is untouched if it's
not the same register as `<SCR1>`. Callsites that need `<IN>` preserved after
CLZ must use a distinct `<SCR1>`. Callsites that immediately do
`sll <IN>, <IN>, <OUT>` right after can use `<IN>` itself as `<SCR1>`.

For each target file:

1. Read the file.
2. Locate the `addi sp, sp, -N` / spill / `call __clzsi2` / reload /
   `addi sp, sp, +N` block.
3. Identify which registers are live at that point and pick 2 dead registers
   for `<SCR1>`/`<SCR2>`. Common free registers: `t0` (x5), `t1` (x6), `t2`
   (x7), `t6` (x31), `x17` (a7). Verify by grep that the chosen scratch is not
   referenced between the end of the CLZ and the next assignment.
4. Replace the push/call/pop block with the inline CLZ template, substituting
   `<IN>` = the register that was `mv x10, <reg>`'d before the call, `<OUT>` =
   the register that received the `mv ..., x10` after the call.
5. If the CLZ was the only `call` in the function, also remove `sw ra, ...` /
   `lw ra, ...` from the outer prologue/epilogue and shrink that frame by 4
   bytes (or 0 if the remaining saved regs already fit).
6. If the file has a two-stage push (two `addi sp, sp, -16` pairs), both
   disappear along with the call they wrapped.

- B: leave the helper, but replace its body with inline CLZ. Simpler change,

Going with **A** because the user asked to optimise for latency.

register pressure - need to verify which registers it saves in its own
prologue and pick scratch from there.

make bench-<func>       # cycle count opt vs ref on FPGA

Base directory for this skill: /home/nick/.claude/skills/commit-optimizer

# Commit Optimizer Skill

Creates well-documented commit messages for optimization work with automatic benchmarking.

## Usage

```
/commit [problem="<description>"] [approach="<description>"]
```

## How It Works

1. **Validates optimization context** — checks that you're in an optimization commit (staged changes present)
2. **Detects what changed** — examines git diff to understand which functions/files were modified
3. **Runs benchmarks** — executes the project's measurement tools (Makefile, commit-size script, or QEMU tests) to capture before/after metrics
4. **Builds commit message** — combines problem statement, approach, and measured results into a structured message

## Commit Message Format

The skill generates messages in this format:

```
optimize: <one-line summary>

Problem:
<explanation of what was slow/large and why it mattered>

Approach:
<description of the optimization technique used>

Result:
<measured improvement with specific metrics>
```

## Examples

**Example 1: Assembly leaf function optimization**
```
optimize: sf_floor.s as leaf function (remove ra push/pop)

Problem:
sf_floor saved and restored return address (ra) unnecessarily, adding 2 instructions (~8 bytes) and extra memory traffic on every call.

Approach:
Eliminated ra push/pop by using tail-call pattern; function now only uses caller-saved registers and doesn't need to preserve ra.

Result:
Code size: 24 → 16 bytes (-33%)
No performance loss (leaf function overhead removed)
```

**Example 2: Register allocation tuning**
```
optimize: sinf.s improve register allocation for FPU operations

Problem:
Register spills were causing extra load/store operations in the hot path of sinf, adding latency on FPU-heavy workloads.

Approach:
Reordered argument reduction to keep intermediate values in registers longer; used callee-saved registers more strategically to reduce spill pressure.

Result:
Code size: 312 → 298 bytes (-4%)
Execution time: 142 → 136 cycles (-4%)
```

## What the Skill Does

- **Auto-detects function** from changed files (e.g., `sf_floor.s` → optimizing `sf_floor`)
- **Runs project benchmarks** to measure size/performance before/after
- **Extracts metrics** from benchmark output (code size, cycle count, etc.)
- **Allows customization** — you can provide problem and approach strings to override auto-detection

## When NOT to Use

This skill is **only** for optimization commits. Use regular git commit for:
- Bug fixes (unless they're performance-related)
- Refactoring without size/performance gains
- Documentation changes
- Adding new features

## Required Parameters (optional)

- `problem` — Brief explanation of what was suboptimal (auto-detected from function changes if omitted)
- `approach` — Description of the optimization technique (auto-detected from git diff if omitted)

The benchmark result is **always** captured automatically.

## Integration with Your Project

This skill is designed for Picolibc RISC-V libm work:
- Looks for Makefile `compare` target to run benchmarks
- Falls back to `commit-size` script if available
- Parses QEMU output or size tool output to extract metrics
- Works with both `-s` assembly files and C implementations in `hardfloat/`

cd /home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
optimize: sf_modf.s inline CLZ to fix latency regression

Problem:
modff was 19% slower than the newlib reference (153k vs 125k cycles) despite
executing slightly fewer instructions. Every call to modff with a non-zero
fractional part hit a push/call __clzsi2/pop block: 6 caller-saved registers
spilled to a second 32-byte stack frame, __clzsi2 called, all 6 reloaded.
This added 12 load/store ops + function-call overhead per invocation. The
outer frame also saved/restored ra unnecessarily.

Approach:
Replace the push/call/pop block with an inline 19-instruction binary-search
CLZ using t6 and x17 as scratch (both dead at that point). Remove the ra
save/restore from the outer prologue/epilogue and shrink the frame from two
16-byte stages to a single 16-byte frame.

Result:
Cycles: 153843 -> 101976 (-34%), speedup vs ref 0.81x -> 1.23x
Instructions (100-call loop): 24260 -> 22700 (-6%)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"
